utterances: It's providing the educational foundation for our children.
That is the most important thing that we can do for them and for ourselves.
The program is expected to cost the state as much as $55 million in the next 10 years, but supporters say the lost revenue will be offset by attracting a better educated workforce.
For NPR News, I'm Susan Sharon in Portland, Maine.
From NPR News, this is ALL THINGS CONSIDERED.
I'm Robert Siegel.
And I'm Michele Norris.
Federal Reserve Chairman Ben Bernanke says problems with subprime mortgages should have only a limited effect on the broader housing market.
But he warns the fallout could be more severe in neighborhoods with a high concentration of foreclosures.
North Minneapolis is one such area: a working-class area where the effort to revitalize the neighborhood has been stalled by the growing number of abandoned houses and for sale signs.
NPR's Scott Horsley reports.
Ruth Hayes(ph) was born and raised on Minneapolis' north side.
She rooted for the Polars at North High School, and she stayed on the north side when she got married, buying a three-bedroom house on Logan Avenue.
She and her husband Gene(ph) had lived in that house for almost 20 years, when Gene was approached by a mortgage broker urging him to refinance.
He just came up to my door.
I said, is that what you do?
Go house to house for in a neighborhood like this?
He said, no, he says, he took a chance on us.
The couple refinanced twice, tapping some of their home's equity for remodeling and a kids' vacation.
What they didn't realize, Ruth says, is they had unwittingly signed up for a mortgage with exploding, adjustable rate.
This guy is talking 50 miles a minute.
Sign here, just initial here, just initial here.
You know he didn't go over it.
Yup, there's an arm - you know, it was too fast.
It was just not right.
Last summer, their payments began to ratchet up.
From $11.82 a month ago to more than $1,500, overwhelming Gene's salary as a city bus driver.
The couple fell behind on their payments, and by early this month, they were in danger of losing their home.
It was scary.
I mean we did a lot of praying.
The Hayeses won a reprieve with the help of ACORN, a nonprofit agency, but many of their neighbors were not so lucky.
Foreclosures in Minneapolis soared nearly 80 percent last year, and state Representative Jim Davnie says those foreclosures are clustered on the city's north side.
When you get those concentrations of foreclosed properties, it moves from being an individual tragedy to a challenge for an entire community.
If you want an example of that, just head here at the corner of 31st Avenue and Sixth Street.
Just to my right is an abandoned house with an unkempt yard and a bright orange condemned sign next to the door.
A no trespassing sign to the right of that has been ignored; somebody has peeled the plywood away form the home's front window.
But it's not just this house.
From here, you can see half a dozen homes that were foreclosed on in the last year alone.
Jess Grinnis(ph) of the Hawthorne Area Community Council says the neighborhood is being hollowed out.
The foreclosures have almost doubled from what we saw last year in Hennepin County, and we don't think the problem is done rising yet.
No single cause is to blame for this epidemic of foreclosures, but Representative Davnie says subprime mortgages pedaled to people with shaky credit are a major factor.
Subprime loans tend to be concentrated in areas like the north side, where many residents are already living close to the financial edge.
It has become an area primarily of color and lower income.
That, then, becomes a target for the predatory lenders.
For them, it was a rich sea to go fishing in, a rich lake.
This is Minnesota.
Sadly, this was a neighborhood where working class homeowners had been building wealth.
Home prices along the leafy, tree-lined streets near downtown had climbed in the last decade to as much as $180,000.
Now, vacant and foreclosed houses are being offered for $60,000 or less.
The longer you're here, the more you're seeing boarded-up houses and your neighbors moving away.
Kim Vaye and her husband have been trying to sell their three-bedroom house since last October, because her work is taking them overseas.
They've already dropped their price by $10,000.
But during two open houses, only two people showed up.
It's hard to sell a house at full price when you have all these houses that the banks own or that people are trying to get rid of at super-low prices.
So you're kind of competing against that to which is really unfortunate.
Area of business is suffering from the exodus as well.
Just three years ago, the opening of the Cub Supermarket was a major boon for the Broadway business corridor.
The sparkling aisles are stocked with food.
But on a recent day, only three of the 14 checkout lines are open.
And store director Ed Anderson is thinking of cutting back on his in-store bakery staff because there aren't enough customers to buy the fresh-baked goods.
(Singing) Heavy seas of love.
The FBI director said his agents could only monitor those kinds of electronic footprints if they got a valid tip about the shooters.
He didn't answer another question about whether the FBI had missed clues the couple in San Bernardino was communicating with terrorist suspects overseas already on the radar of law enforcement.
To find homegrown violent extremists, to find those that are radicalizing and being inspired by these terrorist groups is a very hard thing.
Comey said he needs the help of people in the community including the Muslim-American community to intervene before someone moves toward violence.
And under prodding from senators, he said campaign rhetoric that's alienating those communities is no help to protecting national security.
The FBI also promised to look into hateful threats against Muslims here following attacks in Paris and San Bernardino.
And we want people to know if you think someone is terrorizing or threatening you based on your national origin or your religion, please tell us so that we can investigate that.
We're all in this together.
For now, Comey said he's not aware the Islamic State, which he calls ISIL, has smuggled operatives onto American soil.
We have not seen ISIL cells or networks in the United States.
So far as we can tell, they have not succeeded in penetrating our borders with their operatives.
That's an aspiration of theirs.
We've got to worry about it all day every day.
Comey said he continues to worry about terrorists using encryption to evade detection.

So far, there's no evidence that California shooters used those tools, but the FBI director said one of the men in an unsuccessful attack in Garland, Texas this year exchanged 109 messages with a known terrorist overseas.
And he said because of encryption, he still doesn't know what those messages say.
Carrie Johnson, NPR News, Washington.
The musician Damon Albarn is out with his first solo album.
And if you haven't heard of him, not to worry, the musician himself isn't surprised.
Yeah, I mean I don't think people really know who I am at all, to be honest with you.
I mean, I think they might know of a lot of stuff I've done over the years, but they don't know me.
Here some of the stuff Albarn has done.
the British rock band blur.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""WOOD HOO"")
(Singing) Woo hoo, when I feel heavy...
That is with the British rock band Blur.
Now, how about this one?
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""FEEL GOOD INC."")
(Singing) Feel good...
That's Gorillaz, another British music project.
Damon Albarn gets credit for helping to redefine British rock in the 1990s, ushering in the sound known as Britpop.
Bands like Oasis and The Verve were also part of that genre.
These were British musicians trying to find their own sound different from the American rock of the time.
Damon Albarn points back to a two-month trip he took across the United States.
That was the catalyst for Britpop.
One hundred percent, it was that experience in 1992 in America that created Britpop.
I mean, for me, that's what I was reacting to.
(LAUGHTER)
It's kind of mad that an American experience created something which was interpreted as such a quintessentially English experience.
And so, were you trying to create something that reminded you of England and home and resisting any intuitive change?
Yeah, but I was also - I was, you know, it was a dysfunctional home.
The dysfunctional home he was imagining was Britain as it would look invaded by American culture.
It's a vision he outlined in songs like ""Magic America,"" off the Blur album ""Park Life.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""MAGIC AMERICA"")
(Singing) Buildings in the sky and the air is sugar free...
